paragraphs.text:
%md

# Data Management Assignment 2
## MovieLens Analytics using Apache Spark and Cassandra

This project demonstrates a Python-based data pipeline using Apache Spark and Cassandra. The MovieLens 100K dataset is processed using Spark to perform analytical tasks and store the results in Cassandra for further analysis.

msg.data:
Data Management Assignment 2

MovieLens Analytics using Apache Spark and Cassandra

This project demonstrates a Python-based data pipeline using Apache Spark and Cassandra. The MovieLens 100K dataset is processed using Spark to perform analytical tasks and store the results in Cassandra for further analysis.

print("Hello Shukeri")

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Environment Information

- Python Version: 2.7
- Apache Spark Version: 2.3.0
- Hadoop Distribution: HDP 2.6.5
- Storage Layer: HDFS
- Analytical Database: Cassandra
- Notebook Platform: Apache Zeppelin

This project uses Apache Spark for distributed data processing and Cassandra for storing analytical results.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Project Workflow

1. Load MovieLens datasets from HDFS.
2. Create Spark DataFrames.
3. Clean and preprocess data.
4. Perform analytical tasks:
   - Average movie ratings
   - Top 10 highest-rated movies
   - Active users and favourite genres
   - Users below 20 years old
   - Scientists aged between 30 and 40
5. Store results in Cassandra.
6. Read results back from Cassandra for validation.

msg.data:
Project Workflow

  1. Load MovieLens datasets from HDFS.
  2. Create Spark DataFrames.
  3. Clean and preprocess data.
  4. Perform analytical tasks:
  5. Average movie ratings
  6. Top 10 highest-rated movies
  7. Active users and favourite genres
  8. Users below 20 years old
  9. Scientists aged between 30 and 40
  10. Store results in Cassandra.
  11. Read results back from Cassandra for validation.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Load MovieLens Dataset

The MovieLens 100K dataset consists of three main files:

- u.data : User ratings data
- u.user : User demographic information
- u.item : Movie information and genres

The datasets are loaded from HDFS and converted into Spark DataFrames for analysis.

msg.data:
Load MovieLens Dataset

The MovieLens 100K dataset consists of three main files:

  • u.data : User ratings data
  • u.user : User demographic information
  • u.item : Movie information and genres

The datasets are loaded from HDFS and converted into Spark DataFrames for analysis.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

ratings_df = spark.read.csv(
    "hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.data",
    sep="\t",
    inferSchema=True
)

ratings_df = ratings_df.toDF(
    "user_id",
    "movie_id",
    "rating",
    "timestamp"
)

print("Ratings Records:", ratings_df.count())
ratings_df.show(5)

%spark2.pyspark

users_df = spark.read.csv(
    "hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.user",
    sep="|",
    inferSchema=True
)

users_df = users_df.toDF(
    "user_id",
    "age",
    "gender",
    "occupation",
    "zip_code"
)

print("User Records:", users_df.count())
users_df.show(5)

%spark2.pyspark

items_df = spark.read.csv(
    "hdfs:///user/maria_dev/ml-100k/u.item",
    sep="|",
    inferSchema=True
)

print("Movie Records:", items_df.count())
items_df.show(5)

%md

## Data Quality Check

Before performing analytical tasks, the datasets are inspected to verify record counts and identify any missing values or inconsistencies.

msg.data:
Data Quality Check

Before performing analytical tasks, the datasets are inspected to verify record counts and identify any missing values or inconsistencies.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

print("Ratings:", ratings_df.count())
print("Users:", users_df.count())
print("Movies:", items_df.count())

%md

## Data Preprocessing

The movie dataset contains unnamed columns. Meaningful column names are assigned to improve readability and simplify analytical queries.

msg.data:
Data Preprocessing

The movie dataset contains unnamed columns. Meaningful column names are assigned to improve readability and simplify analytical queries.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

items_df = items_df.toDF(
    "movie_id",
    "title",
    "release_date",
    "video_release_date",
    "imdb_url",
    "unknown",
    "Action",
    "Adventure",
    "Animation",
    "Children",
    "Comedy",
    "Crime",
    "Documentary",
    "Drama",
    "Fantasy",
    "FilmNoir",
    "Horror",
    "Musical",
    "Mystery",
    "Romance",
    "SciFi",
    "Thriller",
    "War",
    "Western"
)

items_df.select("movie_id","title").show(5, False)

%md

## Task (i): Average Rating for Each Movie

This analysis calculates the average rating received by each movie based on all user ratings available in the MovieLens dataset.

msg.data:
Task (i): Average Rating for Each Movie

This analysis calculates the average rating received by each movie based on all user ratings available in the MovieLens dataset.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

avg_rating_df = ratings_df.groupBy("movie_id") \
    .agg(round(avg("rating"),2).alias("average_rating"))

avg_rating_df.show(10)

%spark2.pyspark

task1_df = avg_rating_df.join(
    items_df.select("movie_id","title"),
    "movie_id"
).select(
    "movie_id",
    "title",
    "average_rating"
)

task1_df.orderBy("movie_id").show(10, False)

%spark2.pyspark

z.show(task1_df.orderBy(col("average_rating").desc()).limit(20))

paragraphs.text:
%md

### Interpretation

The average rating for each movie was calculated using all ratings submitted by users. This metric provides an overall measure of audience satisfaction and allows movies to be compared based on their perceived quality. Movies with higher average ratings generally indicate stronger user preference, while lower ratings suggest less favourable reception.

msg.data:
Interpretation

The average rating for each movie was calculated using all ratings submitted by users. This metric provides an overall measure of audience satisfaction and allows movies to be compared based on their perceived quality. Movies with higher average ratings generally indicate stronger user preference, while lower ratings suggest less favourable reception.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Task (ii): Top 10 Movies with Highest Average Ratings

This analysis identifies the ten movies that received the highest average ratings from users. The results highlight the most highly rated movies in the MovieLens dataset.

msg.data:
Task (ii): Top 10 Movies with Highest Average Ratings

This analysis identifies the ten movies that received the highest average ratings from users. The results highlight the most highly rated movies in the MovieLens dataset.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

top10_movies_df = task1_df.orderBy(
    col("average_rating").desc()
)

top10_movies_df.show(10, False)

%spark2.pyspark

movie_stats_df = ratings_df.groupBy("movie_id") \
    .agg(
        round(avg("rating"),2).alias("average_rating"),
        count("rating").alias("number_of_ratings")
    )

top10_movies_df = movie_stats_df.join(
    items_df.select("movie_id","title"),
    "movie_id"
).orderBy(
    col("average_rating").desc(),
    col("number_of_ratings").desc()
)

top10_movies_df.show(10, False)

%spark2.pyspark

z.show(top10_movies_df.limit(10))

msg.data:

paragraphs.text:
%md

### Interpretation

The results show the ten movies with the highest average ratings. Movies with consistently high ratings indicate strong audience appreciation and satisfaction. Including the number of ratings provides additional context, as movies with a large number of ratings and high averages can be considered more reliable indicators of popularity and quality than movies rated by only a few users.

msg.data:
Interpretation

The results show the ten movies with the highest average ratings. Movies with consistently high ratings indicate strong audience appreciation and satisfaction. Including the number of ratings provides additional context, as movies with a large number of ratings and high averages can be considered more reliable indicators of popularity and quality than movies rated by only a few users.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Task (iii): Active Users and Favourite Movie Genres

This analysis identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies. For each active user, the favourite movie genre is determined based on the genre that appears most frequently among the movies they have rated.

msg.data:
Task (iii): Active Users and Favourite Movie Genres

This analysis identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies. For each active user, the favourite movie genre is determined based on the genre that appears most frequently among the movies they have rated.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

active_users_df = ratings_df.groupBy("user_id") \
    .agg(
        count("movie_id").alias("movies_rated")
    ) \
    .filter(
        col("movies_rated") >= 50
    )

print("Number of Active Users:", active_users_df.count())

active_users_df.show(10)

%spark2.pyspark

genre_long_df = items_df.selectExpr(
    "movie_id",
    "stack(19, \
    'Action', Action, \
    'Adventure', Adventure, \
    'Animation', Animation, \
    'Children', Children, \
    'Comedy', Comedy, \
    'Crime', Crime, \
    'Documentary', Documentary, \
    'Drama', Drama, \
    'Fantasy', Fantasy, \
    'FilmNoir', FilmNoir, \
    'Horror', Horror, \
    'Musical', Musical, \
    'Mystery', Mystery, \
    'Romance', Romance, \
    'SciFi', SciFi, \
    'Thriller', Thriller, \
    'War', War, \
    'Western', Western, \
    'unknown', unknown) \
    as (genre, flag)"
).filter(
    col("flag") == 1
)

genre_long_df.show(10)

msg.data:
+--------+---------+----+
|movie_id|    genre|flag|
+--------+---------+----+
|       1|Animation|   1|
|       1| Children|   1|
|       1|   Comedy|   1|
|       2|   Action|   1|
|       2|Adventure|   1|
|       2| Thriller|   1|
|       3| Thriller|   1|
|       4|   Action|   1|
|       4|   Comedy|   1|
|       4|    Drama|   1|
+--------+---------+----+
only showing top 10 rows

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

user_genre_df = ratings_df.join(
    genre_long_df,
    "movie_id"
).join(
    active_users_df,
    "user_id"
)

genre_count_df = user_genre_df.groupBy(
    "user_id",
    "genre"
).agg(
    count("*").alias("genre_count")
)

genre_count_df.show(10)

%spark2.pyspark

task3_df = favorite_genre_df.join(
    active_users_df,
    "user_id"
).select(
    "user_id",
    "movies_rated",
    col("genre").alias("favourite_genre"),
    "genre_count"
)

task3_df.orderBy(
    col("movies_rated").desc()
).show(20, False)

%spark2.pyspark

genre_summary = task3_df.groupBy("favourite_genre") \
    .count() \
    .orderBy(col("count").desc())

z.show(genre_summary)

msg.data:
favourite_genre	count
Drama	372
Comedy	102
Action	72
Thriller	16
Horror	4
Children	2

paragraphs.text:
%md

### Interpretation

The analysis identified users who rated at least 50 movies and classified them as active users. The favourite genre for each active user was determined by counting how frequently each movie genre appeared among the movies they rated. The results show that Drama is the most common favourite genre among highly active users, indicating a strong preference for story-driven and character-focused films. Other genres such as Comedy and Action also appear among some users, highlighting the diversity of viewing preferences within the MovieLens dataset. This analysis provides useful insights into user behaviour and genre popularity among engaged movie viewers.

msg.data:
Interpretation

The analysis identified users who rated at least 50 movies and classified them as active users. The favourite genre for each active user was determined by counting how frequently each movie genre appeared among the movies they rated. The results show that Drama is the most common favourite genre among highly active users, indicating a strong preference for story-driven and character-focused films. Other genres such as Comedy and Action also appear among some users, highlighting the diversity of viewing preferences within the MovieLens dataset. This analysis provides useful insights into user behaviour and genre popularity among engaged movie viewers.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Task (iv): Users Below 20 Years Old

This analysis identifies all users whose age is less than 20 years old.

msg.data:
Task (iv): Users Below 20 Years Old

This analysis identifies all users whose age is less than 20 years old.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

task4_df = users_df.filter(
    col("age") < 20
)

print("Number of Users Below 20 Years Old:", task4_df.count())

task4_df.show(20, False)

%spark2.pyspark

under20_age_summary = task4_df.groupBy("age") \
    .count() \
    .orderBy("age")

z.show(under20_age_summary)

%md

### Interpretation

The results display users whose age is below 20 years old. This group represents younger users within the MovieLens dataset and may exhibit different viewing preferences compared to older age groups. Understanding the demographic composition of users can support further audience segmentation and recommendation analysis.

msg.data:
Interpretation

The results display users whose age is below 20 years old. This group represents younger users within the MovieLens dataset and may exhibit different viewing preferences compared to older age groups. Understanding the demographic composition of users can support further audience segmentation and recommendation analysis.

paragraphs.text:
%md

## Task (v): Scientists Aged Between 30 and 40 Years Old

This analysis identifies users whose occupation is scientist and whose age falls between 30 and 40 years old.

msg.data:
Task (v): Scientists Aged Between 30 and 40 Years Old

This analysis identifies users whose occupation is scientist and whose age falls between 30 and 40 years old.

paragraphs.text:
%spark2.pyspark

task5_df = users_df.filter(
    (col("occupation") == "scientist") &
    (col("age") >= 30) &
    (col("age") <= 40)
)

print("Number of Scientists Aged 30-40:", task5_df.count())

task5_df.show(20, False)

%spark2.pyspark

scientist_age_summary = task5_df.groupBy("age") \
    .count() \
    .orderBy("age")

z.show(scientist_age_summary)

%md

### Interpretation

The analysis identified users whose occupation is recorded as scientist and whose age falls between 30 and 40 years old. This subset of users represents a specific demographic group that may be useful for targeted behavioural analysis or recommendation studies. The results demonstrate how Spark can be used to efficiently filter records based on multiple conditions.

msg.data:
Interpretation

The analysis identified users whose occupation is recorded as scientist and whose age falls between 30 and 40 years old. This subset of users represents a specific demographic group that may be useful for targeted behavioural analysis or recommendation studies. The results demonstrate how Spark can be used to efficiently filter records based on multiple conditions.

paragraphs.text:
## Cassandra Installation Note

Cassandra was not available by default in the HDP Sandbox environment. Following the class notes, the DataStax repository was configured and installation was attempted using yum. However, the repository returned HTTP 404 errors, indicating that the older DataStax community repository is no longer reachable. Therefore, Spark analytical tasks were completed successfully, while Cassandra integration requires an updated installation source or package repository.
